findings.summary: The older and newer versions present the exact same logic for assigning 7th characters, prioritizing cause/intent over place/activity, and sequencing abuse/terrorism events. The text excerpts provided for both versions are identical, indicating no substantive change in the clinical coding rules or reporting requirements between these specific guideline snapshots. The only distinction is the metadata label (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd), suggesting an administrative update or versioning change rather than a logic modification.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign the external cause code with the appropriate 7th character matching the associated injury code. -> icd (New Version): Assign the external cause code with the appropriate 7th character matching the associated injury code.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Sequence external cause codes by priority: child/adult abuse, terrorism, cataclysmic events, transport accidents, then activity and external status. -> icd (New Version): Sequence external cause codes by priority: child/adult abuse, terrorism, cataclysmic events, transport accidents, then activity and external status.

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) contain verbatim identical text regarding the application of external cause codes for diagnoses A00.0-T88.9 and Z00-Z99. There are no clinical logic changes, restrictions, or clarifications between the two provided chunks; they represent the same rule set. The only distinction is the metadata label (version date vs. 'icd'), indicating an administrative update or data synchronization rather than a guideline revision.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): An external cause code may be used with any code in the range of A00.0-T88.9, Z00-Z99, classification that represents a health condition due to an external cause. -> icd (New Version): An external cause code may be used with any code in the range of A00.0-T88.9, Z00-Z99, classification that represents a health condition due to an external cause.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version present the exact same logic for assigning 'Y' or 'N' to external cause codes based on the timing of the event relative to inpatient admission. Both versions explicitly cite the Alphabetic Index and Inclusion/Exclusion notes as the guiding tools and provide identical examples (e.g., falling out of bed at home vs. during hospital stay). There are no substantive differences in the clinical logic, code assignment rules, or specific instructions between the two texts.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign 'Y' for any external cause code representing an external cause of morbidity that occurred prior to inpatient admission (e.g., patient fell out of bed at home, patient fell out of bed in emergency room prior to admission) -> icd (New Version): Assign 'Y' for any external cause code representing an external cause of morbidity that occurred prior to inpatient admission (e.g., patient fell out of bed at home, patient fell out of bed in emergency room prior to admission)
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign 'N' for any external cause code representing an external cause of morbidity that occurred during inpatient hospitalization (e.g., patient fell out of hospital bed during hospital stay, patient experienced an adverse reaction to a medication administered after inpatient admission) -> icd (New Version): Assign 'N' for any external cause code representing an external cause of morbidity that occurred during inpatient hospitalization (e.g., patient fell out of hospital bed during hospital stay, patient experienced an adverse reaction to a medication administered after inpatient admission)

findings.summary: The core logic for sequencing external cause codes based on event chronology rather than injury severity remains unchanged between the two versions. Both guidelines explicitly mandate that combination codes reflect the sequence of events (e.g., fall then impact) regardless of which event caused the most serious injury. The only distinction is a minor phrasing adjustment in the new version regarding the requirement for documentation to 'justify' the use of combination codes, which does not alter the clinical assignment rules.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The selected combination code must reflect the chronological order of events, not the severity of the resulting injury. -> icd (New Version): The selected combination code must accurately reflect the chronological sequence of events, not just the severity of the injury.

findings.summary: The older version established the fundamental rule that external cause codes cannot serve as principal diagnoses. The newer version retains this rule but introduces a critical new restriction prohibiting the simultaneous use of sequela external cause codes with related current nature of injury codes. This addition prevents conflicting coding scenarios where a condition is treated as both a current injury and a sequela, ensuring compliance with accurate coding standards.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): An external cause code can never be a principal (first-listed) diagnosis. -> icd (New Version): An external cause code can never be a principal (first-listed) diagnosis; additionally, a sequela external cause code should never be used with a related current nature of injury code.

findings.summary: The older version (Chunk 317) and the new version (Chunk 745) contain verbatim identical text regarding the sequencing, purpose, and reporting mandates of external cause codes. There are no functional differences in the guidelines provided in these specific excerpts; the new version simply reiterates the same administrative and clinical rules established in the older version.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): External cause codes should never be sequenced as the first-listed or principal diagnosis. -> icd (New Version): External cause codes should never be sequenced as the first-listed or principal diagnosis.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): There is no national requirement for mandatory ICD-10-CM external cause code reporting. -> icd (New Version): There is no national requirement for mandatory ICD-10-CM external cause code reporting.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version present the exact same rule regarding the exemption of external cause codes for specific poisoning scenarios like T36.0X1-. There are no functional changes, clarifications, or reversals between the two texts; the new version merely restates the existing guideline with slightly different phrasing. The logic remains that if the external cause and intent are inherent to the primary code, a separate Chapter 20 code is redundant and should not be assigned.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): No external cause code from Chapter 20 is needed if the external cause and intent are included in a code from another chapter (e.g., T36.0X1-, Poisoning by penicillins, accidental (unintentional)). -> icd (New Version): No external cause code from Chapter 20 is needed if the external cause and intent are included in a code from another chapter (e.g., T36.0X1-, Poisoning by penicillins, accidental (unintentional)).

findings.section: External Cause Code Application for Burns and Corrosions
findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) present the exact same clinical logic, requiring external cause codes to document the source, intent, and place of occurrence for burns and corrosions. The only variations are superficial, consisting of minor phrasing differences in the 'Behaviors' and 'Requirements' lists (e.g., 'Assign' vs 'An external cause code should be used') without altering the underlying coding rule. Since the clinical instruction remains unchanged, this represents an administrative update to the documentation format rather than a substantive guideline change.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign external cause codes to identify the source, intent, and place of the burn/corrosion. -> icd (New Version): An external cause code should be used with burns and corrosions to identify the source, intent, and place of the burn/corrosion.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): External cause codes must be used to document the source, intent, and location. -> icd (New Version): The external cause code must identify the source, intent, and place where the burn or corrosion occurred.

findings.summary: The older version (Chunk 333) and the new version (Chunk 761) contain verbatim identical text regarding the requirement for explicit documentation of undetermined intent. There are no behavioral differences, logic changes, or scope expansions between the two excerpts provided. The only distinction is the metadata label (Taxonomy description) which remains consistent in meaning, suggesting this is a data maintenance update rather than a clinical guideline change.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): External cause codes for events of undetermined intent are only for use if the documentation in the record specifies that the intent cannot be determined. -> icd (New Version): External cause codes for events of undetermined intent are only for use if the documentation in the record specifies that the intent cannot be determined.

findings.summary: The older version provided the core logic for assigning Y38.9 for secondary effects but lacked explicit instructions on code sequencing or the mandatory requirement for Federal Government identification. The newer version clarifies that Y38 must be the first-listed external cause when the FBI identifies the cause as terrorism and mandates the use of additional codes for place of occurrence (Y92.-) and multiple mechanisms. Both versions agree on the restriction against using Y38.9 for initial act injuries and permit combining Y38.9 with other Y38 codes for dual scenarios.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code Y38.9 for conditions occurring subsequent to the terrorist event without specifying sequencing rules. -> icd (New Version): Assign first-listed external cause code from category Y38 when the Federal Government (FBI) identifies the cause of injury as terrorism, with additional codes for place of occurrence (Y92.-).
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Implicitly allows Y38 usage based on event type. -> icd (New Version): Explicitly requires the cause of injury to be identified by the Federal Government (FBI) as terrorism to trigger the use of category Y38 codes.

findings.summary: The core clinical logic regarding the prohibition of Y38 codes and the requirement to classify suspected terrorism injuries as assault remains unchanged between the two versions. The only variation is the addition of a specific requirement in the newer version to ensure the assigned code reflects an assault classification, which serves as a clarification rather than a logical shift. Since the behavioral outcome and the raw text excerpt are identical, this represents a minor administrative refinement to the documentation of the rule.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): When the cause of an injury is suspected to be the result of terrorism a code from category Y38 should not be assigned. Suspected cases should be classified as assault. -> icd (New Version): When the cause of an injury is suspected to be the result of terrorism a code from category Y38 should not be assigned. Suspected cases should be classified as assault.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version contain identical raw text excerpts regarding the default assignment of accidental intent for unspecified causes. There are no behavioral differences, clarifications, or restrictions between the two profiles; they represent the same rule applied in different contexts or versions without modification.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): If the intent (accident, self-harm, assault) of the cause of an injury or other condition is unknown or unspecified, code the intent as accidental intent. All transport accident categories assume accidental intent. -> icd (New Version): If the intent (accident, self-harm, assault) of the cause of an injury or other condition is unknown or unspecified, code the intent as accidental intent. All transport accident categories assume accidental intent.

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) present the exact same clinical logic and coding requirements for complications of care. The text excerpts are nearly identical, stating that specific T codes include the external cause and procedure type, rendering separate codes unnecessary. There are no meaningful differences in behavior, scope, or instruction between the two versions provided.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Some complications of care codes have the external cause included in the code; no separate external cause code is necessary. -> icd (New Version): Some complications of care codes have the external cause included in the code; no separate external cause code is necessary.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version contain the exact same behavioral instruction regarding the assignment of adverse event codes. There is no change in the clinical logic, coding rules, or scope between the two texts provided. The taxonomy descriptions and extracted behaviors are verbatim identical, indicating no substantive update to the feature itself.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): If the same code would describe the causative agent for more than one adverse reaction, poisoning, toxic effect or underdosing, assign the code only once. -> icd (New Version): If the same code would describe the causative agent for more than one adverse reaction, poisoning, toxic effect or underdosing, assign the code only once.

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) contain verbatim identical text regarding the classification of abuse as assault and the requirement to use Y07 codes when a perpetrator is known. There are no functional changes, logic shifts, or behavioral differences between the two excerpts provided; the new version appears to be a direct copy of the older guideline text. Consequently, this represents an administrative update or data duplication rather than a clinical guideline change.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Adult and child abuse, neglect and maltreatment are classified as assault. Any of the assault codes may be used to indicate the external cause of any injury resulting from the confirmed abuse. -> icd (New Version): Adult and child abuse, neglect and maltreatment are classified as assault. Any of the assault codes may be used to indicate the external cause of any injury resulting from the confirmed abuse.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): For confirmed cases of abuse, neglect and maltreatment, when the perpetrator is known, a code from Y07, Perpetrator of maltreatment and neglect, should accompany any other assault codes. -> icd (New Version): For confirmed cases of abuse, neglect and maltreatment, when the perpetrator is known, a code from Y07, Perpetrator of maltreatment and neglect, should accompany any other assault codes.

findings.summary: The two versions provided are identical in content, rules, and raw text excerpts regarding the assignment of Y99 codes. There are no clinical logic changes, guideline reversals, or definition expansions between the 'Older' FY25 Guidelines profile and the 'New Version' profile. The only distinction is the metadata labeling of the chunks, indicating an administrative update where the guideline text itself has remained static.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign a Y99 code whenever any other external cause code is assigned, except for poisonings, adverse effects, misadventures, or late effects. -> icd (New Version): Assign a Y99 code whenever any other external cause code is assigned, except for poisonings, adverse effects, misadventures, or late effects.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Do not assign code Y99.9 if the status is not stated in the documentation. -> icd (New Version): Do not assign code Y99.9 if the status is not stated in the documentation.

findings.summary: The two versions present the exact same clinical logic regarding the assignment of X37.0- for hurricane impacts and X38.- for storm flooding, with no substantive changes to the coding rules. The differences are purely administrative, such as minor phrasing adjustments in the 'New items' section (e.g., 'Explicit instruction' vs 'Use of code') and slight variations in the 'Deprecated' list formatting, which do not alter the operational outcome for coders.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicit instruction to assign code X37.0- for injuries from flooding caused by a levee breaking related to the hurricane. -> icd (New Version): Use of code X37.0- when an injury is incurred as a result of flooding caused by a levee breaking related to the hurricane.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Specific exclusion of code X36.0- for hurricane-related collapses to prevent misclassification of storm surge events. -> icd (New Version): Deprecated: Using code X36.0- for collapses caused by storm surges directly from a hurricane.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical text excerpts and behavioral instructions for assigning external cause codes X37.0- and related codes. The only differences are minor formatting variations in the 'Deprecated' and 'New items' lists, which do not alter the clinical logic or coding rules. Since the core guidelines and raw text are effectively the same, this represents an administrative update rather than a substantive change.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Deprecated list includes 'Assigning code X37.0- for injuries that are clearly not a direct result of the hurricane' -> icd (New Version): Deprecated list includes 'Assigning code X37.0- for injuries that are not a direct result of the hurricane' (removal of word 'clearly').
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): New items list includes 'Explicit instruction to assume hurricane causation when the relationship between the injury and the hurricane is unclear' -> icd (New Version): New items list includes 'Explicit instruction to assume hurricane causation when direct result is unclear' (simplified phrasing).
